question: What is the standard notation for a mixed autoregressive-moving average model of order (p, q)?
options: ARMA(p, q)
ARIMA(p, d, q)
MA(q)
AR(p)
explanation: ARMA(p, q) is the standard notation, where 'p' is the order of the autoregressive part and 'q' is the order of the moving average part.

question: Which of the following equations correctly represents an ARMA(1,1) process?
options: Z_t = φ₁Z_{t-1} + θ₀a_t + θ₁a_{t-1}
Z_t = φ₁Z_{t-1} + θ₁a_{t-1}
Z_t = φ₁Z_{t-1} + a_t + θ₁a_{t-1}
Z_t = a_t + θ₁a_{t-1} + θ₂a_{t-2}
explanation: The general form of an ARMA(1,1) process is Z_t = φ₁Z_{t-1} + a_t + θ₁a_{t-1}, where a_t is the white noise shock at time t.

question: A time series is modeled as an ARMA(2,1) process. What does this imply about its structure?
options: It depends on its own two previous values and the weighted average of the last two shocks.
It depends on its own two previous values and the shock from one prior period.
It depends on its own single previous value and the shock from one prior period.
It is the weighted average of the last two shocks and the shock from one prior period.
explanation: An ARMA(2,1) process has an autoregressive component of order 2 (depends on Z_{t-1} and Z_{t-2}) and a moving average component of order 1 (depends on a_{t-1}).

question: Under what condition is an ARMA(p, q) process considered stationary and invertible?
options: The roots of the AR characteristic equation must lie inside the unit circle, and the roots of the MA characteristic equation must lie outside the unit circle.
The roots of both the AR and MA characteristic equations must lie inside the unit circle.
The roots of the AR characteristic equation must lie outside the unit circle, and the roots of the MA characteristic equation must lie inside the unit circle.
The process is always stationary and invertible by definition.
explanation: For stationarity, the roots of the autoregressive polynomial must lie outside the unit circle. For invertibility (so the MA part can be expressed as an AR(∞)), the roots of the moving average polynomial must lie outside the unit circle.

question: What is a key advantage of using a parsimonious ARMA(p, q) model over a pure AR(p) or MA(q) model?
options: It often requires fewer parameters to achieve an equally good fit to the data.
It guarantees the model will be both stationary and invertible.
It is simpler to estimate and forecast with.
It eliminates the need for model identification and diagnostic checking.
explanation: Parsimony is a key principle in time series modeling. An ARMA model can often capture complex dynamics (like those of a high-order AR or MA process) using a combined model with a lower total number of parameters (p + q).
